2john_truth.title: The Elect Lady -- Do Not Receive Deceivers
2john_truth.synopsis: 'The elder to the elect lady (eklekte kyria) and her children, whom I love in truth' (v.1). The greeting is trinitarian: 'Grace, mercy, and peace will be with us, from God the Father and from Jesus Christ the Father's Son, in truth and love' (v.3). John rejoices that 'some of your children are walking in the truth' (v.4) and repeats the commandment: 'that we love one another' (v.5) -- 'this is the commandment, just as you heard from the beginning, so that you should walk in it' (v.6). Then the warning: 'For many deceivers (planoi) have gone out into the world, those who do not confess the coming of Jesus Christ in the flesh. Such a one is the deceiver (ho planos) and the antichrist (ho antichristos)' (v.7). 'Watch yourselves, so that you may not lose what we have worked for, but may win a full reward' (v.8). 'Everyone who goes on ahead and does not abide in the teaching of Christ, does not have God. Whoever abides in the teaching has both the Father and the Son' (v.9). The practical instruction: 'If anyone comes to you and does not bring this teaching, do not receive him into your house or give him any greeting, for whoever greets him takes part in his wicked works' (vv.10-11). John closes: 'I have much to write to you, but I would rather not use paper and ink. Instead I hope to come to you and talk face to face, so that our joy may be complete' (v.12). 'The children of your elect sister greet you' (v.13).

key_verse.text: For many deceivers have gone out into the world, those who do not confess the coming of Jesus Christ in the flesh. Such a one is the deceiver and the antichrist.

hebrew_terms: eklekte kyria (elect lady -- v.1; either a specific woman named Kyria, or [more likely] a personification of a house church: the 'chosen lady' is the congregation, her 'children' are its members, and her 'elect sister' [v.13] is another church)
planos (deceiver -- v.7; from planao [to wander/lead astray]; one who causes others to deviate from the truth; the same root used for 'deceitful spirits' [pneumasin planois] in 1 Tim 4:1)
ho antichristos (the antichrist -- v.7; the definite article intensifies: this is THE antichrist spirit manifested in those who deny the incarnation; the term appears only in 1 John and 2 John)

proago (to go on ahead/go beyond -- v.9; from pro [forward/ahead] + ago [to lead]; those who 'progress' beyond Christ's teaching into novel doctrines actually leave God behind; 'advancement' that abandons the gospel is apostasy)

didache tou Christou (the teaching of Christ -- v.9; the body of apostolic doctrine about Christ; the standard by which all teaching is measured; to 'abide' in it means to remain faithful to the original gospel)

koinonei tois ergois autou tois ponerois (shares in his wicked works -- v.11; koinoneo = to partner/share; offering hospitality to false teachers makes one complicit in their deception; the cosmic implications: you become an ally of the antichrist spirit)
2john_truth.ane_backdrop: In the first-century world, traveling teachers depended on hospitality from local congregations (cf. 3 John 5-8; Didache 11-12). A house church that hosted a teacher was implicitly endorsing that teacher's message. By refusing hospitality to false teachers, the community protected itself from deception and withdrew logistical support from the false teaching's spread. This was not rudeness but responsible boundary-keeping in a world where endorsement was conveyed through hospitality.

second_temple.source: Didache 11:1-2 (Teaching of the Twelve Apostles, c. 100 AD)
second_temple.summary: 'Whoever comes and teaches you all these things previously mentioned, receive him. But if the teacher himself turns and teaches another doctrine to contradict these things, do not listen to him.'
second_temple.relevance: The Didache confirms the early church practice of testing traveling teachers against apostolic doctrine -- exactly what John commands. The hospitality gate-keeping function was standard practice.

cross_refs.note: 'Test the spirits... every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God' -- the same incarnation test in fuller form
'Many antichrists have come' -- the same antichrist warning expanded
The positive counterpart: Gaius receives true missionaries with hospitality
'Watch out for those who cause divisions and create obstacles contrary to the doctrine that you have been taught; avoid them'
'Even if we or an angel from heaven should preach a gospel contrary to the one we preached to you, let him be accursed' -- Paul's parallel boundary
'As for a person who stirs up division, after warning him once and then twice, have nothing more to do with him'

2john_truth.divine_council_note: 2 John makes explicit the cosmic implications of hospitality to false teachers: 'whoever greets him takes part in his wicked works' (v.11). In the divine council framework, the false teachers are not merely confused humans but agents of 'the deceiver and the antichrist' (v.7) -- the hostile spiritual power that opposes Christ. To welcome these agents is to become 'partners' (koinoneo) in the cosmic rebellion. The test remains christological: does the teacher confess that Jesus Christ 'has come in the flesh' (v.7)? The incarnation is the divine council's central strategy -- the Most High entering enemy territory in human form. Denying the incarnation is not a minor theological error but a direct assault on the council's plan of salvation. John's boundary instruction reflects the reality that the cosmic war has practical consequences: which teachers you support determines which side of the conflict you stand on.

sections.heading: Greeting and the Command to Love (vv. 1-6)
sections.body: The Elder addresses the 'elect lady' -- likely a house church personified as a woman (cf. 'elect sister' in v.13 = another church). He rejoices that some of her 'children' walk in truth (v.4). The command 'from the beginning' is love for one another (v.5). Love is defined as 'walking according to his commandments' (v.6) -- not sentiment but obedience. This establishes the positive foundation before the negative warning.

sections.heading: The Deceiver and the Antichrist (vv. 7-11)
sections.body: 'Many deceivers have gone out into the world' (v.7) -- the same language as 1 John 4:1. The specific denial: they do not confess 'the coming of Jesus Christ in the flesh.' Such a person is 'the deceiver and the antichrist' (v.7). The warning: 'Watch yourselves' (v.8) -- apostasy is a real danger, and one can 'lose' what has been worked for. Anyone who 'goes on ahead' (proago) beyond Christ's teaching does not have God (v.9). The practical boundary: do not receive false teachers or even greet them (vv.10-11). This is not about personal rudeness but about cosmic allegiance: hospitality to false teachers is complicity with the antichrist spirit.

sections.heading: Closing: Face to Face (vv. 12-13)
sections.body: John prefers personal conversation to written correspondence: 'I hope to come to you and talk face to face, so that our joy may be complete' (v.12). The letter is a stopgap until personal visit is possible. 'The children of your elect sister greet you' (v.13) -- greetings from another church, reinforcing the network of connected communities under John's pastoral oversight.
